segments.title: USS Independence exposes the Great Frigate tradeoff
segments.summary: America's first commissioned ship of the line becomes a case study in why impressive heavy frigates could not substitute for fleet battle power.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke introduces Independence as both the first US Navy ship of the line to serve and its only razee. He uses her to expose the fleet-level weakness hidden by the celebrated Great Frigates. Those heavy frigates were excellent for presence missions, long cruises, and single-ship actions, but they cost too much to supply all the cruisers a navy needed. They also had to withdraw whenever a British third rate or larger ship appeared. Independence therefore illustrates why a balanced navy needed affordable ordinary frigates alongside ships able to stand in the line of battle.

evidence.note: The introduction identifies Independence's two distinctions and compares ships of the line with the Great Frigate procurement choice.

segments.title: National potential is not the same as deployable naval power
segments.summary: British assessments focused on the American government's fielded forces rather than the country's population, industry, or future potential.
segments.body: The lecture separates criticism of early US defense policy from judgments about the American people. The country possessed population, industry, commerce, and enormous future potential, but foreign governments assessed what its government could deploy at that moment. In 1812 Britain could compare fewer than a dozen American frigates with hundreds of Royal Navy warships backed by decades of wartime finance and experience. That order-of-magnitude gap shaped British calculations regardless of American bravery or technical skill. The watch point is useful for distinguishing latent national resources from organized military capability.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke distinguishes national potential from government capability and compares the two navies' scale.

segments.title: Impressment and neutral trade before the War of 1812
segments.summary: Conflicting citizenship rules, weak documentation, Caribbean trade, and blockade law made impressment a tangled dispute rather than a single clean grievance.
segments.body: British law treated British-born sailors as subjects even after American naturalization, while American naturalization papers often could not establish a sailor's status. The US Navy also used impressment on a smaller scale, and pay bounties could turn impressed Royal Navy sailors into nominal volunteers. Neutral merchants meanwhile carried French colonial goods through American ports, sometimes only claiming to have landed the cargo, and both belligerents seized ships trading with the enemy. American lists of alleged impressment cases contained duplicates and hearsay, making negotiation harder. The rejected Monroe-Pinkney settlement shows how close the governments came to resolving most issues without war while leaving the 1756 trade rule and a precise citizenship test unsettled.

evidence.note: The transcript explains naturalization, impressment records, neutral trade, seizures, and the proposed treaty settlement.

segments.title: Indigenous trade, Canada, and the war's competing motives
segments.summary: British arms-for-furs commerce, mistaken expectations of Canadian support, and expansionist calculations all fed the decision for war.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats weapons supplied to Indigenous communities mainly as merchant trade for furs rather than a simple British state plot, while recognizing its effect on American security. He also stresses that Quebecois inhabitants, Loyalist refugees, and newer British settlers were unlikely to welcome an American invasion. Those demographics undermined the belief that an advance into Canada would trigger a friendly uprising. Britain was heavily occupied with Napoleon, however, which made the moment appear favorable to American expansionists. The resulting picture is a maelstrom of genuine disputes, domestic politics, strategic misreading, and opportunism rather than a war with one tidy cause.

evidence.note: The lecture covers the arms trade, Canadian demographics, American procurement ideas, and rival interpretations of Madison's aims.

segments.title: Mobile gunboats promise coastal defence on the cheap
segments.summary: The proposal to move single-cannon boats by horse and carriage captures the procurement thinking that left the United States without a balanced fleet.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke highlights a plan to replace much of the seagoing navy with small gunboats that could be transported to threatened points by horse and carriage. The scheme promised economical local defence without funding expensive ships and permanent crews. In practice, it could not provide the endurance, concentration, or sea control of a balanced fleet. The later British attack on Washington supplies the lecture's pointed test of that assumption. This short watch point connects institutional reluctance to spend with the operational weakness exposed in wartime.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the proposal to replace the navy with single-cannon boats moved where needed by horse and carriage.

segments.title: Commercial growth does not automatically create naval credibility
segments.summary: Britain respected expanding American commerce while assigning France, Spain, Russia, and the Napoleonic struggle far greater strategic priority.
segments.body: American Atlantic trade had expanded dramatically, and British observers recognized the United States as a serious commercial power. That economic success did not make its small navy a leading military threat in British planning. France, Spain, Russia, and the wider Napoleonic War remained much higher priorities despite alarmist newspaper commentary about American growth. A large British-born share of the American merchant workforce also tangled commerce with impressment when seized vessels reached Admiralty courts. The passage shows why wealth and shipping volume only become strategic leverage when institutions convert them into credible power.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke contrasts rapid American trade growth with Britain's strategic priorities and explains how British-born merchant sailors complicated seizures and impressment.

segments.title: Great Frigate victories meet the Royal Navy blockade
segments.summary: American frigates won celebrated actions, but British ships of the line progressively confined the US Navy and exposed its missing fleet tier.
segments.body: The heavier American frigates had decisive advantages over ordinary British frigates and earned important early victories. That freedom narrowed sharply once British ships of the line arrived in strength and could dominate the approaches to major ports. The blockade did not need to seal every mile of coastline because Maine fell under British control and substantial parts of the American population opposed the war. British crews could even buy fresh provisions from communities near the ports they were supposedly blockading. This strategic constraint, rather than the outcome of isolated duels, forced Americans to recognize that frigates alone were insufficient.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke contrasts frigate victories with blockade pressure and the realization that the navy lacked ships of the line.

segments.title: America repeatedly starts but rarely completes battle fleets
segments.summary: Continental, Columbus, Independence, Pennsylvania, Delaware, and later programs reveal a persistent gap between American ambition and completed ships.
segments.body: USS America went to France, several planned vessels disappeared, and only nine of twenty-eight started American ships of the line were completed for US service. The abortive Columbus and later programs show how often authorization and framing failed to produce commissioned warships. Dr. Clarke argues that a modest balanced force of ships of the line, heavy frigates, and ordinary cruisers would have represented American wealth and intent more accurately. Such a fleet could keep part of its strength in ordinary while maintaining an active squadron. Pennsylvania's 130 guns and the quality of the Independence class demonstrate that technical capability was not the limiting factor.

evidence.note: The transcript surveys planned and completed American ships of the line and proposes a sustainable balanced fleet.

segments.title: The Independence class shows American shipbuilding quality
segments.summary: Franklin's gun decks, Washington's covered slip, and Columbus's sail plan display a sophisticated industrial base when funding was sustained.
segments.body: The sister ships look like powerful 74-gun third rates despite carrying heavier nominal batteries. Franklin's two complete gun decks show the family's line-of-battle form, while Washington's covered building slip reveals substantial infrastructure investment. Columbus's sail plan was arranged to draw useful power from each sail and suggests careful naval architecture. These were not crude emergency vessels produced by an incapable industrial base. Dr. Clarke's criticism is therefore institutional: American projects were often completed to a very high standard or abandoned altogether.

evidence.note: The lecture uses the class's hulls, covered construction, and sail plans to assess American industrial quality.

segments.title: A powerful blockade keeps Independence in Boston
segments.summary: Several British 74s, the 98-gun Boyne, frigates, and smaller vessels made a sortie by the newly armed Independence strategically reckless.
segments.body: Independence launched and armed rapidly in 1814, but the Royal Navy knew she was coming and assembled overwhelming force off Boston. The squadron could include three to five 74-gun third rates, the 98-gun second-rate Boyne, frigates, and smaller vessels. Taking the new American ship and Constitution against those odds would have been strategically reckless. The example shows how Britain varied blockade strength according to the threat inside each port rather than distributing ships evenly. Dr. Clarke connects that practice to long Royal Navy experience monitoring European coasts and the Atlantic routes shaped by winds and currents.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the Boston blockade force and explains why Independence did not challenge it.

segments.title: Blockade searches combine strategy with anti-slavery enforcement
segments.summary: Royal Navy searches served both the blockade system and a genuine British campaign against the transatlantic slave trade, creating friction with neutral states.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke places searches of American shipping within Britain's wider experience of monitoring long coasts and ocean chokepoints. From 1807 the Royal Navy also searched vessels as part of suppressing the transatlantic slave trade, and it did not readily accept neutral objections. Other countries could see that enforcement as maritime bullying and as a convenient reason to inspect suspicious traffic during the Continental blockade. The lecture does not reduce the policy to either pure humanitarianism or pure strategic cover. Instead, it argues that genuine anti-slavery zeal and naval utility operated together, which helps explain both British persistence and foreign resentment.

evidence.note: The transcript links blockade experience, searches of American ships, suppression of the slave trade, foreign objections, and mixed strategic and moral motives.

segments.title: HMS Nymphe's fireship raid nearly destroys Independence
segments.summary: British sailors and marines turned a captured sloop into a fireship and sent it toward the nearly completed American ship of the line.
segments.body: HMS Nymphe's sailors and marines captured a sloop inside Boston Harbor, set it alight, and sent it toward the nearly completed Independence. The attempt failed because defenders kept the burning vessel away from the new ship of the line. Even so, the raid demonstrates detailed British knowledge of Independence's location, construction schedule, and readiness. Destroying her before commissioning would have removed a future fleet threat at very low cost. It also would have allowed the Royal Navy to reduce the unusually strong blockade force maintained off Boston.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke describes the captured sloop, fireship attack, defenders, and British preventive logic.

segments.title: Independence adapts the transatlantic 74-gun design tradition
segments.summary: Her hull and rig reflect an American interpretation of British adaptations to the original French 74, optimized for oceanic service.
segments.body: The French 74 supplied the starting point, while British practice widened and provisioned the type for long global deployments. The British interpretation traded some peak speed for stability, stores, and the ability to arrive ready to fight after an ocean passage. Independence carried that design lineage into American construction with strong hull lines and a capable full rig. Dr. Clarke therefore describes her as an American interpretation of a British adaptation rather than an isolated national invention. He also flags the attractive story about using trees previously marked by Admiralty surveyors as explicitly unverified.

evidence.note: The lecture traces the design lineage and explicitly cautions that the marked-timber story is unverified.

segments.title: What did Independence's ninety 32-pounders actually mean?
segments.summary: A mixed battery of long guns, medium guns, and carronades better fits the ship's pierced ports and crew than ninety identical long guns.
segments.body: Although often listed as carrying ninety 32-pounders, that label does not specify gun type. A 32-pounder could be a long gun, a medium cannon, or a carronade, with very different weight and manpower demands. Comparing sister Franklin's documented battery with Independence's 74 hull ports suggests long guns below, medium guns above, and carronades on the upper works. That arrangement reconciles the nominal gun total with a hull built as a third rate. A crew of roughly 790 also appears too small to operate ninety long 32-pounders while sailing and supporting the ship.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke compares gun types, Franklin's documented mix, hull piercings, and manpower requirements.

segments.title: Ship counts and steam claims need source criticism
segments.summary: Conflicting totals for American ships of the line and a false break between sail and steam show why apparently authoritative reference pages need checking.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke pauses over a reference that claimed the United States built fifteen ships of the line when his vessel-by-vessel count produced nine completed for American service. Including the Great Frigates would not repair the total because they were not ships of the line. The same source attributed the end of new construction to steam making the type useless. Early steam battleships, however, included converted ships of the line, so propulsion did not create such a simple categorical break. The passage is a compact lesson in checking definitions, totals, and technological-transition claims against the underlying ships.

evidence.note: The transcript challenges a fifteen-ship total and the claim that steam simply made ships of the line obsolete, noting early steam battleship conversions.

segments.title: A Barbary show of force begins Independence's service
segments.summary: Independence led Bainbridge's 1815 Mediterranean squadron as a visible demonstration that the United States could now deploy battle-fleet power.
segments.body: Stephen Decatur had already compelled peace with the Barbary States, so Independence's arrival reinforced the settlement rather than fighting another campaign. Leading William Bainbridge's squadron, she visibly demonstrated that the United States could now send line-of-battle power into the Mediterranean. She returned to Newport in November 1815 and continued as a flagship under Bainbridge and then John Shaw. Congressional underfunding limited opportunities to send her to sea and placed her in ordinary from 1822 to 1836. Those reserve years nevertheless preserved an expensive hull for later conversion and decades of additional service.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Bainbridge's Mediterranean deployment, return, flagship duty, and years in ordinary.

segments.title: Razeed into one of the US Navy's best frigates
segments.summary: Removing one deck in 1836 produced a fast, powerful 54-gun frigate but cost the navy one of its very few ships of the line.
segments.body: Removing a deck in 1836 reduced Independence to a 54-gun razee and created one of the US Navy's fastest and most powerful frigates. A razee retains the expensive hull strength and sailing qualities of a ship of the line in a lower, handier cruising form. That performance explains the appeal of conversion, but also why navies did it sparingly: they sacrificed a costly battle-fleet unit. Independence left the line just as Pennsylvania entered service, keeping the United States near a ceiling of only seven or eight ships of the line. The decision favored an excellent distant-station flagship over already scarce line-of-battle strength.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke explains the razee conversion, its performance benefits, and its effect on US battle-fleet numbers.

segments.title: Independence serves diplomacy, commerce, and the Mexican War
segments.summary: As a razee flagship, Independence carried a minister to Russia, protected South American commerce, blockaded Mexico, and landed forces at Mazatlan.
segments.body: Her 1837 voyage linked Portsmouth, Copenhagen, Kronstadt, and Rio while carrying Minister George Dallas to Russia and then joining the Brazil Squadron. Commodore John Nicholson's unsupported attempt to mediate between France and Argentina illustrates the limits of a prestigious flagship without government authority or overwhelming national power behind it. Recommissioned for the Mexican-American War, Independence blockaded the coast, helped capture vessels, and landed sailors and Marines to occupy Mazatlan. She then cruised to Hawaii before returning east and later served as the first American warship to show the flag at Spezia. The razee's assignments demonstrate the diplomatic reach, commerce protection, and amphibious utility that justified retaining a large cruising flagship.

evidence.note: The lecture follows the ship's Russian, Brazilian, Mexican War, Pacific, and Mediterranean assignments.

segments.title: A long receiving-ship career ends in destructive salvage
segments.summary: After Pacific flagship service, Independence spent more than fifty years at Mare Island before being burned in 1915 to recover metal fittings.
segments.body: The ship entered Mare Island Navy Yard in 1857 and remained a receiving ship until decommissioning in 1912. By then her rig and upper works had changed so much that she no longer resembled the powerful sailing warship shown earlier in the lecture. A proposal to use her as a restaurant for the Panama-Pacific International Exposition failed when organizers withheld a permit. Salvagers removed pig iron, ballast, valuable hardwood, and fittings before burning the hull at Hunter's Point in 1915 to recover metal. Destroying the first commissioned American ship of the line and the navy's only razee erased an exceptional physical link to the early fleet.

evidence.note: The transcript covers receiving-ship duty, failed exposition plans, salvage, and burning at Hunter's Point.

segments.title: Independence's larger lesson: reserves, hard power, and credibility
segments.summary: The ship's long usefulness supports investment in reserve battle fleets and shows how credible hard power changes diplomacy even when it does not prevent war.
segments.body: Years in ordinary did not make Independence wasteful; they preserved an expensive capability that could return for diplomacy, war, and distant stations. Dr. Clarke avoids claiming that prewar American ships of the line would certainly have prevented the War of 1812, because the conflict had too many causes and communication failures for that certainty. He argues instead that even six Columbus-class ships would have changed foreign assessments, alliance possibilities, and the Royal Navy commitment required for blockade. Credible force also might have reduced the insecurity and search for slights that he associates with lacking the recognized attributes of a significant naval state. The conclusion connects fleet strength with confidence, deterrence, and the hard power needed to make legitimate demands credible while still requiring attention to the other side's interests.

evidence.note: The conclusion assesses reserve value, counterfactual fleet strength, alliance possibilities, deterrence, and diplomatic credibility.

segments.title: A battle fleet could have made America a useful ally against France
segments.summary: Madison considered conflict with France, and American ships of the line might have opened alliance and colonial possibilities unavailable to a frigate-only navy.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke notes that James Madison at one stage considered war with France rather than Britain. A United States able to contribute ships of the line would have looked more attractive as a coalition partner during the Napoleonic Wars. Britain wanted some French colonies removed from French control without necessarily annexing them or handing them to another European rival. An American ally with credible naval force might therefore have received opportunities that were politically unavailable to European powers. The counterfactual is deliberately presented as a possibility, not a prediction, and illustrates how force structure can change diplomatic options before a battle occurs.

evidence.note: The transcript considers Madison choosing war with France and the possibility that a stronger American fleet could have enabled alliance and colonial arrangements.
